+        Promise.resolve({ success: false, stdout: '', stderr: 'pack error', all: 'pack error', failed: true })
When packing packages to a path, it turns out it may be useful or necessary to group layers of the tree together rather than using sequential ordering.

This PR adds a new option `packStyle: 'sequential' | 'layer'`. The new option refers to packages from each layer of the graph that can be published in parallel. The first layer will be the leaf packages with no dependencies, and the last will be the root packages that depend on all others. The layers go in numbered subfolders under `packToPath`.

The layering uses [Kahn's algorithm for BFS](https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/dsa/topological-sorting-indegree-based-solution/) to extract the layers in O(V+E). (Apparently LLMs are good at choosing and writing algorithms thanks to the many code examples online...)

If possible, layers are computed based on only the set of published packages. This should be safe with beachball's default behaviors of bumping dependents, but layers will be computed over the full graph under any of the following conditions which might cause missing edges. (Not 100% sure this is necessary, but not sure how to disprove it either...)

In either case, `devDependencies` are ignored when computing the graph since they can't break published packages. This should improve improve efficiency and potentially condense the resulting layers slightly.
I also replaced usage of the `toposort` package with the new layer logic, since they effectively do the same thing (exact ordering may vary, but the topological relationships are handled either way).

Currently, there's only VERY basic cycle handling: all cycles are grouped together on a final layer, regardless of any interdependencies. This should be fine since the `toposort` package previously used by beachball doesn't handle cycles at all, which implies that repos publishing with beachball don't have cycles. (Tarjan's strongly connected components algorithm could be added at the end to break cycles into more layers if needed in the future, but it's a lot of extra complexity.)","Implement layer pack style

742,34e95610ddf21216a5a2bd312bd222c4ee9b8f92,9228d48eb0420c4f608dc65f335e30fa571a84bf,If a package had only invalid change files (packages that were private or didn't exist), beachball would start the publish process but then fail partway through because there was nothing to commit. `getBumpInfo` had attempted to filter out those files, but by that point it was too late because the publish process had already started.
This PR fixes the issue at the root by modifying `readChangeFiles` to exclude (and warn about) any change files from private or nonexistent packages.

762,90259249ebcc0ed63a9bc57f73dc9f5ad5b1de88,90259249ebcc0ed63a9bc57f73dc9f5ad5b1de88,Some of beachball's tests cover individual helper functions, but were being run as part of E2E tests because they need to use fixtures on the filesystem. The problem with this is that if one of the helper functions has a bug, that failure will probably also cause downstream failures in the actual E2E scenario tests, and then logs for the important failure in the helper function will be buried in logs for all the downstream failures.

This PR introduces a third category, functional tests, which are run after unit tests and before E2E tests. I moved some of the existing ""E2E"" tests into the functional group and added one new test.
NOTE: The files which show as deleted are not actually gone
column2: they're just split into multiple new files.","Separate functional tests from E2E tests

984,d111ab85b5a2750ce3cfca58962e58b6e0283f40,d111ab85b5a2750ce3cfca58962e58b6e0283f40,Fix an edge case and improve changelog tests.

Scenario: Suppose there's a changelog group with `masterPackageName: 'foo', changelogPath: 'packages/foo'`. This is supposed to make the grouped changelog be written in place of `foo`'s normal changelog. But if `foo` has no changes besides dependency bumps, and there are no changes in other grouped packages (so the grouped changelog isn't written), previously the dependency bumps from `foo`'s normal changelog would be prepended to what's supposed to be a grouped changelog. (See [this test case](https://github.com/microsoft/beachball/pull/984/files#diff-1819960692d0b38e963fb83251d573780176b3d9a0923f04a27de0a7190cd209R384) for a clearer example.)
This PR fixes the issue by *always* skipping potential grouped changelog paths when writing package changelogs.

I also updated the tests for `writeChangelog` to have more complete coverage/be more realistic, remove redundant tests, and not rely so heavily on snapshots, which are too easy to ignore if they change when they shouldn't.","Prevent accidentally writing dep bumps to grouped changelog
